speaker: interviewer
text: --just telling us how in 1939 your parents were denounced and arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- maybe I should start, preclude with something else that when the Germans occupied Czechoslovakia in March, on March 15, 1939, they proclaimed the western provinces, what is today the Czech Republic, Bohemia, and Moravia, for the protectorate of Bohmen und Mahren in German. And the Slovacks proclaimed a separate free state under-- under Tiso who was a priest, very anti-Semitic. The name is T-I-S-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were separate and free, the free state of Slovakia. And this was the protectorate with a protector and there were-- right away there were-- certain laws were being proclaimed, you know. It was always at-- at every corner they would post it-- the Germans would post in German and Czech. For the general population, I'm not talking now about the Jewish laws.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then slowly but surely, the-- the Jews were-- had to give away their radios, their jewelry, their-- but these laws came very slowly, after the war was declared. Then it happened with a-- with a vengeance. But after 1939, you couldn't go to a normal school anymore. And that's why I went to this language school where I stayed in 1939 and '40. And it was-- it was a long way from home. And I-- you were asking me about Jewish girls. Around the corner from me lived another girl who came from a convent school. She was not Jewish. And we went together to this school. We had to change twice the street car. And it was a heartbreaking experience for my mother. Little did she know what expect her. And then I came home from school on the 3rd of December 1939. And I told you already in the previous tape. And it was awful. We didn't know where they were. My mother's two brothers-- one was my father's partner, my uncle Otto, and the other brother was my Uncle Rudolph who lived also very close to us. They were there because [? Manya ?] called them. And they didn't know what happened at first, you know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and why they took also my mother. And we had no news. It was-- it was really awful. And then my aunt and my mother's cousins, two cousins who were the the-- the two sisters, and they were sort of the heads of the family. And they-- they wanted me to move to one or to-- to the other. And I couldn't stay alone. And [? Manya ?] said, she can stay alone. I was staying with her. And I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they couldn't force me. They-- they-- you know, it was-- the times were such that they couldn't force even a child to do what-- what they wanted, which was unheard of, you know, even a year before that I wouldn't obey. But I just said, I won't go. So-- and they had enough on their shoulders anyway so to be saddled with another girl. So I went to my Aunt Elsa, for-- to my mother's sister, whom I loved. And I stayed there for two, three nights and then I said look, myself, this is ridiculous. It will take me another half an hour to get to school. They lived very far, further than us. And I want to be at home. What if-- what if they will give a message, or what if something happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: And nothing happened, and nobody gave a message. They arrested my Uncle Otto two weeks later. It was around Christmastime. They arrested him still before Christmas. And-- and then I came home from school sometimes in February, I was already 13. My mother was home. They let her go. And she went into this prison a young woman. She was, after all, born in '96. You know, this for '39, '40 now. She came home an old lady. A really old lady. She was small. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe smaller than I am, probably. She had dark black hair, dark hair. And she was round, you know, ladies were not slim. They didn't-- no Weight Watchers. She was skinny. Everything was falling off her. She was old. She was shaking. She was white-haired. And she wouldn't tell me anything. It was-- it was very, very sad. That was-- that was awful. And Manya was still with us. So she could-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she could at least-- and there was still food to be had-- she could still cook. And she couldn't eat anyhow. In-- and by that spring, she somehow pulled herself together and-- I don't know. She-- it was my mother who instigated my joining this [? El Al. ?] She said you can't sit at home alone and mope. This is ridiculous. And you have to have some young people outside of school. Mind you, the school where I went to was extremely stimulating. There were four Jewish girls there, aside from me, maybe five. Maybe six. Two were from out of town. Funnily enough, two of those girls who were twins roomed with me in Terezin afterwards. And they survived. And we had English and French. That was all. From 8:00 to 1:00, divided. And it was very intensive. Very, very intensive. French, I knew. I spoke French. But-- so I was in an advanced class for literature and grammar and all that. They-- it was drilled into us. And Madame-- and that's where I got my-- my English base. You know, I-- it certainly was very, very good because that's what stayed with me. It wasn't very difficult for me when we came here. Not at all. But French was difficult because I didn't understand them. I thought I spoke French and they-- I didn't understand what in stores or everywhere. They understood me but I-- it took me a while before I got used to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You're referring to the French-Canadian dialect.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes. And so that was a very good sort of education that I got there. It wasn't only the languages. It was the literature. It was everything. And the teachers who were Gentile-- Madame was French from France. She was our principal French teacher. And the others were British, I don't know what they did there. I think they were wives or-- of Czechs who happened to stay there. And it was a very well-run school and very strict school. And there was-- as far as I remember, and I would have remembered, there was no-- no anti-Semitism. But at the end of the school year, in 1940, we were told that we cannot come back. That government, the-- the German authorities, don't allow it anymore. So that one year was a Godsend. But my mother decided I needed something else because these girls lived all over Prague. You know, it was impossible in the afternoon to get to them or to get together with them. And she felt I needed some young people. That I was brooding too much, reading too much, studying too much. And I had to get out. And she met somebody who told her, why doesn't she join this [? El Al ?] group which is five minutes from where we lived. They meet there and they are young people. And they are all from good families. And my daughter goes there or my niece goes there. So I went, not expecting anything, and I was happy as a lark. From day one on, we met twice a week. And then-- and then when I couldn't go to school in summer we-- of 19-- First of all I should retract. My father came back in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: May of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of 1940. He came back. One day, my uncle came back and about two weeks later, my father came back. Now, that's how our personal life started to change. My uncle had an apartment. While he was in jail, it was confiscated. Everything in it too. It was lock, stock, and barrel. He had nowhere to go. And we still had this large apartment, so he moved in with us. That-- that wasn't bad, you know. So-- and [? Manya ?] had to go. Jews were not allowed to have servants whatsoever anymore. So [? Manya ?] went. And-- I was very happy in this [? El Al ?] group. And then we started to go for Sunday trips. We were still allowed to go out of Prague, not for very long. But still, that year we still could go for-- for trips to the country and to be-- for two weeks, we spent in a-- at a farmer's. And he let us-- we-- we helped him with the harvest. And he did us-- he gave us a little bit bread and butter. And we were alone. We were happy. Nobody bothered us. But when I returned to Prague, so the-- the-- the whole schlimazel started. There was-- and there were different laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: In September, you started to wear the Star of David. And-- by know, we had no radios. No-- no-- no jewelry. You know, you had to give it up. You had-- what else? We couldn't go to parks. We couldn't go to cinemas. We couldn't go to theaters. We couldn't go-- we had to-- there were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was rationed. And you got different rations than the others. Then you couldn't shop when the-- when the Gentiles shopped. You could shop only for the leftovers. As far as I-- you know, this is very hazy. Only the-- the main things are not hazy in my mind. The main thing was that you-- they moved all of the Jewish population from all over Prague into this quarter, where we happened to live. You know, which is, if you go today to Prague, so it's called the Old City. Which was, in the-- in the Middle Ages, it was the Prague ghetto. And there is this old Prague cemetery. And there are the two shuls, the-- the oldest. And that's where-- and that's where they established the office of the Jewish community, which took the orders from the German [? commandator, ?] from the German command. Bought-- and they distributed, they issued the laws, you know. And the Jews that they were moving from all over Prague, they had to be placed in apartments, in existing apartments, in this particular quarter. And before long in our-- in our apartment there was my mother, my father, my uncle. There was another couple with two children. And there was a lady with a single girl. And another bachelor. The bathroom certainly, it wasn't adequate. And nothing was adequate. The-- the kitchen, the cooking. I was-- it was terrible. And we slept, the four of us, in our dining room. Because it was the-- the largest room. So-- I mean, I slept, but it was not the most pleasant of things. Then-- and then, I mean, I-- I was running away from home, sort of. I was-- I was-- started my romance, as you call it, with [? Rudolf. ?] I fell head over heels in love.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother screamed at me that she will cut off this-- there is a famous book by Czech author, which is called You know, but fairy tale meaning not-- not about a fairy tale but it's a love story. So she would yell at me, I'll-- and scream at me, I'll cut off your fairy-- [? may ?] fairy tale. You will never do that. And you are crazy. And what are you thinking about? And all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was the 8 o'clock curfew, you know. And then I started to work with children. I just couldn't-- I had to do something. I couldn't go to school. I couldn't study. So you had always at the Jewish, at this community service, you-- you could find a job. And I started to work in a-- with a-- in a little nursery, as a helper. And there was this little boy named Danny. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we used to-- it's so funny. When you walk now through Prague, this-- this nursery was diagonally across from where we lived in a-- a rather modern building on the top floor. And every noon-- we would, there was a communal kitchen. And two of us would walk for their lunch. And that's about taking another five minutes or maybe eight minutes. And now when I walk through Prague, it's all there. It didn't change. You know, it's-- it's-- it's-- it's difficult to relate to that time. We would pick up their lunch and feed them lunch. And I loved this Danny. And I-- I sort of made up my mind that if I ever have a boy, he'll be Danny.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- that was Danny. And then in-- I think it was already in October of '40, they-- yes, it was in the fall of '40, they called the first transport. There was no Terezin yet. They called twice, two and a half thousand people. They-- they were told just to take 50 kilos with them, anything that they carry. They had to assemble at the Prague Exhibition Grounds, which was a-- a big sort of glass palace which had a floor, sort of a warehouse down. It was not on the outskirts of Prague. It was really basically in a very good part of Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they just stood there and-- in two days, they were transported to Lodz, the Lodz ghetto. And out of those 5,000 that were transported in '40, 11 came back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I know a girl who came back. She lives in Calgary now. And so when you-- that was the first inclination we had that there will be-- that we'll be transported somewhere. And then they called-- then they-- and how they called, and you got sort of a piece of paper like a telegraph strip where you said you should present yourself there and there with the 52 kilos that you can carry. Or maybe it was just your name and your assigned number. I don't think it's-- the 50 kilos it was issued by the-- by the offices of the Jewish elders. And then there was nothing for six months. And then they took young men, which was called AK1 and AK2. And we knew where they were going because we heard from them. They were-- they went to Terezin. And they were preparing Terezin, which is in old fort. It was a garrison town. It started as a garrison town under [? Maria-Therese ?] which is before the French Revolution. It has ramparts, real ramparts until now. And there were barracks. And there was always army stationed there, under from [? Maria-Therese ?] on, even under Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they moved-- they emptied all the barracks. There was no Czech army anyway under the Germans. And these are guys who went there, these 1,500, they prepared. They built the bunks. They prepared the barracks for the invasion of the inmates. So we knew where they were. So then the transports started to go to Theresienstadt. So everybody said, that's not too bad thing. And it's And it's only 60 kilometers, 60 miles or whatever north from Prague. Everybody knew where it was. And we are close. And we are still on the territory of Bohemia. We are not in the Sudetenland. And-- and the-- the-- the young men who were there and who were building were allowed to write a post card. And they could-- the families could send them a parcel. But then the transports started to go, two or three always. Everybody was scared of it. And then you heard that after about two, three months, there were very little communication. They always collected 1,000 people and sent them to work in the east. But nobody knew where. What this east was? Where they send them? What-- and everybody dreaded it. It was really-- that was the big scare. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the overcrowding, not the living together, not the shortages of food. But this-- that you go to Terezin and then they would transport you to somewhere in the east. That was-- that was really-- that was sitting on everybody like a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother started to dye sheets and collect-- buy, put away her good clothes and-- and good books and-- and whatever jewelry she had left. And put it-- pack it and give it to some Gentile friends who were supposed to keep it for her over the war. One person returned something. Only one. Nobody else did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then they arrested my father again, in the middle of this, all this. But we were still in Prague. And-- and we didn't know where he was. It's was-- One day we-- somebody let us know that he's going, being interviewed in-- in Prague. And I waited there until he came out. And my uncle and they were really beaten up. It was awful. It was really awful. That-- that was terrible. And other than that, my-- after the nursery, I started to work, help out in a-- a home for newborns in Prague, which was somewhere else. And I loved it. And when we-- and when we had time, [? Rudolf ?] and I did terrible things. We took away our Stars of David and put them on little safety pins and went to a movie or went to the outskirts of Prague and took a swim. And it was-- you know, you couldn't do these things. It was everything what-- you would pay for it with your life. We just didn't believe-- we were completely reckless. We felt nothing will happen to us. We have a guardian angel. Nothing happened to us, as a matter of fact. But if my mother would have known that-- I think she had an inkling-- but she-- she would have been desperate. And we met at all kinds of places. It was crazy. That was crazy what we did. I wouldn't do it today. But it was fun. At least we had something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we-- we were so scared that we'll be separated. That he'll go with a transport, and I'll go with another transport. And what are we going to do? And we'll never see each other again. We were really-- that-- that drove us to desperation. But it turned out fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then while my father was in jail, we were called into this transport, which was a penalty transport. First, they killed [? Heidrich. ?] That was, I described that in the book. That was-- that was an eerie situation because we were returning from a trip to the country without the Stars of David when this-- this whole announcement came. And there was martial law, and if you didn't live through martial law in an occupied city, you don't know what-- what it is. And that night-- I mean, we parted, and we were very scared. And I ran home. And that night, I slept in the kitchen and a guy came in with our neighbor, who was at that point German. And he-- he just scared the people who were living there. He didn't want anything. And he-- he just wanted to see our papers, and whether everybody who is in this house is accounted for, who is-- and whether we are supposed to be there. And we-- he left. And then he-- he said, you will all be sent away anyway, and I'll took this apartment over. It's just the right kind of apartment for me and my family. Because we are in a small apartment across the hall from you, and we won't stay there. And the people started to get notices who lived with us to go into the transport. But we were still there. And then there was another-- they-- and they called the guys who-- who did this attempt at on [INAUDIBLE]. They were hiding in a basement in a church. And somebody denounced them. And they-- they-- so many-- thousands of people lost their lives because of this one man who was really terrible, when you-- historically when you look at him. He was a terrible figure but-- it cost too much-- too many lives were paid for this one person. Anyhow, in-- in October we got-- until then, we were supposedly protected, my mother and I, because my father was in jail in Prague. So they didn't like to-- it was their crazy idea that families shouldn't be separated. So he-- we were in a way protected from the transport. But only until a certain point because then there was another penalty transport-- I forget for what-- we were called in. And [? Rudolf ?] wasn't and his parents weren't. And we were desperate. And he found out from some of his friends that this is a penalty transport which will stop in Terezin for a day but nobody will be allowed to leave. And they will go-- they will all go east.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to the Gestapo and said that I was pregnant. And that he wants to go with me. And they let him go. And he marched. We were sitting there on the floor in this exhibition ground which incidentally are now the National Gallery of Impressionist Art in this palace. They just opened it. They re-did it and it's beautiful. Eva, we're going to need to take a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in a moment.
